Negative Appraisals.value: I can't rely on other people
I will never be able to feel normal emotions again

Threat.value: Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively
Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience

vignette:
This 30-year-old man from Oceania presents with persistent posttraumatic stress symptoms following a suicide in his close life, with a current PCL-5 score of 46 indicating a significant burden. In his day-to-day life he is troubled by unpredictability, and certain sounds stand out sharply to him, especially babies crying and loud noises. He describes these as deeply uncomfortable experiences and often finds himself wanting to get away from them.

His account is marked by a bleak view of himself and of other people. He says he cannot rely on other people, worries that he will never be able to feel normal emotions again, and describes feeling dead inside. He speaks about these thoughts in a flat, resigned way, as though they have become part of how he understands his life. There is a pronounced emotional distancing in the way he talks about others and about himself.

He remains irritable and prone to angry outbursts, and he notices strong physical reactions when something reminds him of the stressful experience. He also reports feeling jumpy and easily startled, with a heightened sense that his body is constantly primed. In social settings, he tends to keep others at a distance and is often guarded, which leaves his relationships thin and unsatisfying. As a single man, he has few close supports to draw on.

He has narrowed his routine, avoiding unpredictable situations and steering away from environments where babies or loud noises are likely to be present. Even so, the sense of deadness and mistrust remains prominent, and his day-to-day life feels increasingly constrained. The combination of harsh appraisals, emotional numbing, irritability, and physical hyperarousal continues to leave him struggling to function with any sense of ease.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
This 30-year-old man from Oceania presents with persistent posttraumatic stress symptoms following a suicide in his close life, with a current PCL-5 score of 46 indicating a significant burden. Since the loss, he has become increasingly unsettled by everyday unpredictability, and particular sounds now carry a heavy charge for him. Babies crying and loud noises are especially difficult to tolerate, often leaving him tense and on edge in situations that others would experience as ordinary.

A central theme in his account is a growing loss of trust in people around him. When he is confronted by unexpected events or disturbing sounds, he quickly finds himself thinking that he cannot rely on other people. At the same time, he worries that he will never be able to feel normal emotions again and describes himself as feeling dead inside. These appraisals sit close to the surface of his experience and appear to shape how he interprets both his inner world and the people around him.

In day-to-day life, he remains irritable and prone to angry outbursts, especially when he feels physically activated by reminders of what happened. He also reports being jumpy and easily startled, with strong bodily reactions that can be hard for him to settle. His emotional distance has made him less available to others, and he tends to keep interactions brief and guarded. As a single man, he has increasingly limited his social contact, and relationships now feel strained and fragile.

Over time, this pattern has narrowed his life. He avoids situations that feel unpredictable and is particularly uneasy around babies or in environments where loud noises are likely. Despite this withdrawal, his sense of deadness and mistrust remains intact, and he continues to experience himself as changed in a permanent way. The combination of strained trust, emotional numbness, and persistent physiological arousal maintains a distressing cycle that keeps his world feeling unsafe and difficult to re-enter.

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly says that when confronted by unexpected events or disturbing sounds, he becomes unsettled and interprets the situation as unsafe/on edge; this makes the trigger produce threat appraisal directly, which is forbidden.
violations.quote: When he is confronted by unexpected events or disturbing sounds, he quickly finds himself thinking that he cannot rely on other people.

vignette_attempts.feedback:
- Triggers → Threat: The vignette explicitly says that when confronted by unexpected events or disturbing sounds, he becomes unsettled and interprets the situation as unsafe/on edge; this makes the trigger produce threat appraisal directly, which is forbidden.
  quote: "When he is confronted by unexpected events or disturbing sounds, he quickly finds himself thinking that he cannot rely on other people."

vignette_attempts.vignette:
This 30-year-old man from Oceania presents with persistent posttraumatic stress symptoms following a suicide in his close life, with a current PCL-5 score of 46 indicating a significant burden. Since the loss, everyday unpredictability has become especially hard for him to tolerate, and certain sounds now stand out sharply in his awareness. Babies crying and loud noises are particularly distressing to him, and he describes these moments as intensely difficult to sit through.

His account is marked by a bleak view of himself and of other people. He says he cannot rely on other people, worries that he will never be able to feel normal emotions again, and describes feeling dead inside. These beliefs are now woven into how he talks about his life, giving his experience a closed and hopeless quality. He appears emotionally shut down in many interactions, with less capacity to feel connected or settled.

He remains irritable and prone to angry outbursts, and he notices strong physical reactions when something reminds him of the stressful experience. He also reports feeling jumpy and easily startled, with a heightened sense that his body is constantly primed. In social settings, he tends to keep others at a distance and is often guarded, which leaves his relationships thin and unsatisfying. As a single man, he has few close supports to draw on.

He has narrowed his routine in response to these difficulties, avoiding unpredictable situations and steering away from environments where babies or loud noises are likely to be present. Even so, the sense of deadness and mistrust remains prominent, and his day-to-day life feels increasingly constrained. The combination of harsh appraisals, emotional numbing, irritability, and physical hyperarousal continues to leave him struggling to function with any sense of ease.

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly says that since the loss, everyday unpredictability has become especially hard for him to tolerate and certain sounds are distressing, which directly links triggers to increased threat/distress.
violations.quote: Since the loss, everyday unpredictability has become especially hard for him to tolerate, and certain sounds now stand out sharply in his awareness. Babies crying and loud noises are particularly distressing to him.
